metadata.summary: Apply Nielsen Heuristic #8 to classify UI elements as Primary/Secondary/Tertiary/Remove, using visual hierarchy and progressive disclosure to reduce cognitive load.

content.title: Aesthetic and Minimalist Design
content.summary: Cluttered interfaces increase cognitive load, slow task completion, and bury important content in visual noise. Every extra element added to a screen dilutes the relative visibility of every other element. Apply Nielsen Heuristic #8: classify all elements as Primary (essential for task completion), Secondary (helpful but not critical), Tertiary (nice-to-have, rarely needed), or Remove (no value). Use progressive disclosure for Tertiary content and visual hierarchy to guide users to what matters. Define the screen's single primary task before auditing; all classification is relative to that task.

why.p: Every additional element on a screen competes with Primary elements for attention. This is not aesthetic preference but an empirically measured attention cost. Minimalist interfaces reduce decision fatigue, speed first-click success, and lower error rates.

The constraint of minimalism also forces product discipline: removing rarely-used features exposes which features actually matter. When designers cannot add features without removing something else, priority decisions become explicit rather than implicit accumulation.

list.item: Evaluating a near-finished UI for visual noise and clutter before shipping.
Auditing a feature that has accumulated too many options after incremental additions.
Generating prioritized element-removal recommendations from a screenshot or design spec.
Pre-launch heuristic sweep as part of a broader usability review.
After each major feature addition — lightweight regression check for creeping complexity.
Data-dense tools (analytics dashboards, trading platforms, IDEs) where density is required by the domain.
Early exploration or wireframing phases — minimalism audits are premature without final content.
When the root problem is information architecture, not visual presentation — fix IA first.
As a substitute for user testing — agent opinions on "noise" are not user behavior data.

rule.statement: Define the screen's single primary task before starting any audit. Every element classification (Primary/Secondary/Tertiary/Remove) is relative to that task. An element that is essential for one task may be Tertiary or Remove for a different task on the same screen.
rule.rationale: Without a defined primary task, "minimalism" is arbitrary aesthetic preference. With a defined task, classification becomes testable: does this element help the user accomplish [primary task] right now? Elements that do not are candidates for removal or hiding.

rule.statement: Classify every element as Primary (essential for task completion), Secondary (helpful but not critical), Tertiary (rarely needed, nice-to-have), or Remove (no value to any user). Apply the appropriate action: keep visible, visually subordinate, hide behind progressive disclosure, or delete.
rule.rationale: Every additional element competes with Primary elements for attention. Nielsen's heuristic #8 is not about aesthetics — it is about attention economics. More elements equals less visibility for each element, slower task completion, and more errors.

rule.statement: Show summaries; reveal details on demand. Hide advanced options, secondary information, and rarely-used features behind expandable sections or secondary screens. Never hide Primary-tier content behind progressive disclosure.
rule.rationale: Progressive disclosure reduces visible complexity without removing functionality. Users who need advanced features can access them; users who do not are not burdened by them. Applied incorrectly (hiding Primary content), it creates friction for the majority of users and increases task completion time and error rates.

rule.statement: Establish visual hierarchy using size, color saturation, position, and whitespace — in that priority order. Every element should be visually subordinate to the elements above it in the content hierarchy.
rule.rationale: When everything has equal visual weight, nothing reads as primary and users must read everything to determine what matters. Visual hierarchy guides the eye to what matters first. Size and saturation carry the most attention weight; position and whitespace reinforce hierarchy without adding visual complexity.

list.item: Size: larger equals more important. Primary action button is larger than secondary.
Color saturation: high saturation draws attention. Primary action uses brand color; secondary is outlined or muted.
Position: top-left is seen first (LTR). Primary content above the fold. Destructive actions at bottom or secondary positions.
Whitespace: more space equals emphasis. Primary elements have more breathing room.

rule.statement: Before hiding an element, ask: what happens if we remove it entirely? If the answer is "nothing noticeable," remove it. If it is "some users lose access to an edge case," hide it. If it is "users lose a primary function," reorganize or shrink it — never hide Primary-tier elements.
rule.rationale: Hidden elements still exist in the cognitive model of users who know about them. True minimalism removes elements that add no value; hiding is for elements needed by some users but not the majority. The distinction matters for product decisions: removal is final, hiding preserves access.

rule.statement: Too minimal is also a failure mode. When users cannot find features they need, when essential actions are hidden, or when the interface feels empty, add appropriate content and surface critical actions. Minimalism is purposeful inclusion, not sparseness.
rule.rationale: The goal is that every element earns its place, not that the minimum number of elements exist. An overly stripped interface fails users by hiding what they need. Validate removal recommendations against analytics (page views, click rates) and task completion time before implementing.

rule.statement: If users complain they cannot find features, if task completion time increases after a simplification, or if key actions have low discovery rates in heatmaps, restore visibility before shipping further removals.
rule.rationale: Minimalism serving the product team's aesthetic preferences at the cost of user task success is not good UX — it is design without validation. Analytics and usability testing are the arbiters, not the designer's preference for clean screens.

example.title: Google homepage
example.code: Primary task: Search Elements shown: logo, search input, two buttons (Search / Feeling Lucky) Everything else: removed from homepage entirely Result: fastest time-to-task-completion of any major product homepage

example.title: Apple product page
example.code: Primary: Large product image (dominant) Secondary: Product name + key feature headline Tertiary: Specs, accessories, comparison (further down page) Remove: No competing products, no navigation clutter above hero

example.title: Medium article view
example.code: Primary: Article content (full width, readable typography) Secondary: Author info, clap button (floating, not competing) Tertiary: Related articles (after end of content) Remove: No sidebar ads, no pop-up navigation, no competing content

antipattern.description: Keeping all features visible at once out of fear of removing anything.
antipattern.reason: Feature accumulation over time turns every screen into a toolbar of options. Each added feature dilutes the visibility of existing features. Regular content audits against the primary task prevent accumulation.

antipattern.description: Decoration that does not inform: background images, gradient overlays, animated elements that carry no information.
antipattern.reason: Decorative elements consume visual attention budget without returning information value. They compete with functional elements and slow visual scanning. Every visual element should serve the primary task or be removed.

antipattern.description: Equal visual weight for unequal elements: all buttons the same size, all text the same weight.
antipattern.reason: When everything has the same visual weight, users must read everything to determine what matters. Visual hierarchy guides the eye and reduces cognitive load. A primary action button must be visually dominant over secondary actions.

antipattern.description: Hiding primary functionality behind "More" menus or progressive disclosure.
antipattern.reason: Progressive disclosure is for Tertiary content. Hiding Primary or Secondary content increases task completion time and error rates. If users regularly have to expand "Show more" to complete the primary task, the Primary content is wrongly classified as Tertiary.

antipattern.description: Desktop-level information density applied to mobile layouts.
antipattern.reason: Mobile screens are 4-6x smaller than desktop. Elements that read as "appropriately dense" on desktop become visually overwhelming on mobile. Mobile layouts require stricter hierarchy and more aggressive use of progressive disclosure. Apply the audit on mobile first — it forces honest prioritization decisions.

rule.statement: Agent cannot see actual visual rendering — it works from element lists or HTML. Provide enough context (element type, label text, position) to make useful judgments. Agent cannot reliably identify which elements users actually need without analytics or user research data.
rule.rationale: Static snapshots miss important context like state changes (tabs, accordions, modals), element visibility on different viewports, and actual user behavior from analytics.

rule.statement: Without usage data, the agent will over-recommend removal of infrequently-named but critical edge-case actions (e.g., "export as PDF"). Human review of each recommendation is required before shipping.
rule.rationale: An agent seeing "export" once in a feature list may flag it as Tertiary when it is actually Primary for power users. Designer judgment is required to weigh frequency against importance.

rule.statement: Agent may flag accessibility-required elements (skip links, ARIA landmarks) as "visual noise" — always cross-check against accessibility audit before implementing remove recommendations.
rule.rationale: Minimalism and accessibility sometimes conflict; legal/compliance requirements may override aesthetic preferences.

rule.statement: Do not auto-apply agent-generated recommendations — produce a recommendation report for designer review. Validate removal recommendations against product requirements before shipping.
rule.rationale: Agent analysis is advisory; design decisions require human judgment on business context, compliance, and user needs.

template.purpose: Content audit table: element, purpose, user need, priority (P1-P3), action (keep/remove/hide); sections for visual elements and information density.
